Q1: I tried to keep a steady routine, mostly studying in the evenings after I finished my part-time work. I would set aside time to listen to lectures and go through materials, usually about four hours a day spread across the week. With six online courses, I had to be organized or I'd fall behind completely. I kept a simple notebook where I wrote down what needed to be done each week, though honestly I didn't always get through everything on my list.
Q2: I tried to be regular, but life doesn't always cooperate when you're managing limited resources. Most weeks I'd study on the same evenings, but when deadlines piled up or when I had extra shifts at work, everything shifted. I'd end up cramming more hours into weekends or staying up late. The pattern was supposed to be consistent, but it often became reactive to whatever was most urgent.
Q3: Usually I logged in when I knew there was a new lecture posted or when I needed to check assignment deadlines. Sometimes I'd get a notification and that would remind me to go in. I also logged in just to feel like I was staying connected to the course, even if I wasn't always doing something productive. It became a habit to check in, almost like checking messages.
Q4: Yes, after the first few weeks, logging in became automatic for me. I'd wake up, have my coffee, and open the LMS on my phone just to see what was new. It happened because I was taking so many courses online that the LMS basically became my classroom. The gamified tasks they introduced actually helped make it feel more like a routine because there were small goals to hit.
Q5: The video lectures were the most useful for me because I learn best by listening. I could play them while doing other things or replay parts I didn't understand the first time. The discussion boards were also helpful because hearing other students' perspectives sometimes clarified things better than the materials alone. I spent over 200 minutes watching videos because that's genuinely how I absorb information best.
Q6: I often skipped the reading materials, especially the long PDFs. With limited data and an older laptop, downloading and reading them was frustrating. I also didn't engage much with some of the optional quizzes because they felt like extra work when I was already struggling to keep up with the basics. If something wasn't directly tied to my grade, it often got pushed aside.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see how it connected to passing the course or understanding something I'd been confused about. The gamified tasks were actually motivating because they gave me small wins and made progress feel tangible. If something felt like busywork or wasn't clearly explained, I'd lose interest quickly. I needed to see the point.
Q8: Optional activities always felt like a luxury I couldn't afford. When you're juggling six courses with limited time and energy, you focus on what's required first. Required tasks had weight because they affected my grade directly. Optional ones might have been interesting, but they felt like extras that I could only do if everything else was somehow magically finished.
Q9: Absolutely, I did a lot of studying that never showed up in the system. I'd download lecture audio and listen to it on my commute or while cooking. I also borrowed textbooks from a friend and studied from those. My notebook was full of handwritten notes that I reviewed constantly, but none of that activity would ever register anywhere online.
Q10: There were many times I'd be sitting with my notes, replaying concepts in my head, or discussing course material with a classmate over coffee. None of that shows up in any log. I'd also sometimes pause a video, think deeply about what was said, and not touch the computer for an hour while I processed it. Learning isn't always clicking and scrolling.
Q11: Yes, often. I'd watch a video once online to make sure it worked, then download the audio portion to listen to repeatedly offline. Same with some PDF slides—I'd access them once, print them at the library, and then study from the paper copies for weeks. One access doesn't mean one study session; it could mean dozens of reviews offline.
Q12: There were definitely harder periods, especially mid-semester when everything seemed due at once. I was feeling quite sad during parts of the course, dealing with some personal stuff that made it hard to focus. My resilience isn't great, so when things piled up, I'd sometimes just shut down emotionally and struggle to engage with anything academic.
Q13: Workload and fatigue were constant battles. Working part-time while taking six courses meant I was always tired. Sometimes I'd log into the LMS with good intentions but just stare at the screen because my brain was too exhausted to process anything. Competing responsibilities definitely affected how much energy I had left for actual learning.
Q14: Yes, there were times I'd scroll through materials or have a video playing but my mind was completely elsewhere. I was physically present on the platform but mentally checked out. It's hard to admit, but sometimes logging in was more about feeling like I was doing something rather than actually learning.
Q15: I postponed assignments more than I should have, even knowing they were important. Part of it was feeling overwhelmed and not knowing where to start. My assignment completion rate was pretty low because I'd get paralyzed by the stress of having so much to do. It's easier to avoid something than face the anxiety of possibly failing at it.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my engine. Without them, I probably wouldn't have done anything. I'd often wait until a deadline was close before really engaging with the material for that assignment. It's not ideal, but the pressure of a deadline was sometimes the only thing that could cut through my avoidance and get me moving.
Q17: Feedback had a mixed effect on me. When I got positive feedback, it motivated me to keep going. But when feedback was critical or I got a low score, it sometimes made me want to disengage because it confirmed my fears that I wasn't good enough. I tried to use feedback constructively, but emotionally it was hard.
Q18: The course structure gave me a framework to follow, which I needed. Having clear modules and weekly topics helped me know what to focus on. But sometimes the structure felt rigid when life got complicated. The gamified elements they added were actually helpful because they broke things into smaller, manageable pieces.
Q19: The gamified tasks made engagement easier because they gave me immediate feedback and small rewards for progress. What made it harder was when instructions were unclear or when there were too many things due at once. Also, not having great internet sometimes made accessing materials frustrating, which killed my motivation.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is trying hard but struggling to produce quality work, or when they're anxious and keep checking things without actually completing them. Maybe they're engaged with learning but not confident enough to submit. Or they could be like me sometimes—present but too overwhelmed to finish what they started.
Q21: That makes sense to me because someone might be studying effectively offline or through other means. They could be efficient learners who don't need to spend hours on the platform to understand the material. My attendance was low and my assignment completion wasn't great, but I still passed because I understood the core concepts through my own study methods.
Q22: A course log can show when you logged in and what you clicked on, but it can't show whether you actually understood anything or how hard you were trying. It misses all the offline studying, the emotional struggles, the thinking that happens away from the screen. It captures activity, not learning or effort.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely caring about understanding the material, not just going through the motions. It's when you're mentally present and curious, when you want to learn rather than just pass. For me, engagement feels like being pulled into the content rather than pushing myself through it. It's rare but meaningful when it happens.
Q24: The LMS data wouldn't show how sad I felt during parts of this course, or how much I struggled with motivation despite logging in regularly. It wouldn't show the hours I spent listening to downloaded lectures on the bus, or the conversations with classmates that helped me understand difficult concepts. My engagement was real, just not always visible.
